retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Any of the first three standard tests can be used to screen for type 1 or type 2 diabetes. For gestational diabetes, an oral glucose tolerance test is used with different glucose load and cut-off values than in non-pregnant adults. When HbA1c is being measured, it is noteworthy that some hemoglobin variants can interfere with certain essays of HbA1c and should be taken into account. If the patient was diagnosed with diabetes using lab testing (HbA1c or fasting/postprandial blood sugar) without having clinical symptoms, it is essential to send a second test to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: If negative for prediabetes, it should be repeated at least every three years. [4] It might be repeated sooner if they develop symptoms or the risk profile is higher.  Patients with prediabetes are tested yearly. Patients diagnosed with HIV should be tested as soon as possible. Women diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus must undergo lifelong screening at least every three years.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: These patients are classified as stage A: At risk for heart failure. The suggested screening uses BNP or NT-proBNP using cutoff values of 35 pg/mL or 125 pg/mL, respectively. If patients screen positive, patients are classified as Stage B: Pre-Heart failure and should be referred to a cardiovascular specialist (Class 2b recommendation). In addition, clinicians should ask patients about the symptoms of these diseases in routine visits.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: If diabetes screening tests are negative, the first-degree family members of patients with type 1 diabetes may be offered screening for the risk of developing type 1 diabetes with one of the islet cell autoantibodies. [3] This testing should be coupled with diabetes symptoms education and close follow-up, which might help with early identification.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperlipidemia screening is done using blood tests in either fasting or non-fasting state. Universal pediatric screening at age 9 to 11 and repeated at age 17 to 21 is important for diagnosing familial hypercholesterolemia. For patients with a history of smoking, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and a family history of premature atherosclerotic disease, screening should begin at age 20 if they were not tested between ages 17 and 21 years as per the pediatric universal screening guidelines. For patients without the above risk factors, screening can begin at age 35 for men and 45 for women. For ASCVD risk stratification and intervention, LDL cholesterol values are most relevant. If screening results are normal, the testing can be repeated every five years. It can be repeated sooner (every year) in patients with abnormal values near the intervention threshold.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Type 2 Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Other: acanthosis nigricans, polycystic ovary syndrome, history of coronary vascular disease, physical inactivity [11] [7] The recommendations for those with a high risk of developing diabetes are candidates for more early/frequent screening, early behavior intervention, and intensive treatment. The recommended interval for the screening of asymptomatic patients is 3 years. The interval can be short in patients who have obesity and other major risk factors for developing diabetes. The American Diabetes Association recommends screening for diabetes in adults aged 45 years or older and screening in persons with multiple risk factors regardless of age. The American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists, American Academy of Family Physicians, Diabetes Australia, Diabetes UK, and the Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care have recommendations that differ in their approach to screening but acknowledge and stress risk factors compared to an older age.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: For people presenting with SBP ≥180 and/or DBP ≥120, hypertension can be diagnosed at the first measurement. Similarly, hypertension can be diagnosed if SBP ≥ 160 and/or DBP ≥ 100 PLUS evidence of end-organ damage (hypertensive retinopathy, ischemic cardiovascular disease, left ventricular hypertrophy). [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Prediabetes is not a clinical entity itself; however, it often serves to identify people at increased risk of developing diabetes and cardiovascular disease. [5] Prediabetes is diagnosed using one of the three methods: Fasting plasma glucose 100 to 126 mg/dl A 2-hr postprandial glucose 140 to 199 mg/dl after 75 gm glucose load Hemoglobin A1C 5.7 to 6.4% Some entities describe prediabetes as fasting plasma glucose between 110 to 126 mg/dl. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Evaluation -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Women diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) should have lifelong testing at least every three years. For all other patients, testing should begin at age 45 years, and if results are normal, patients should be tested at a minimum of every 3-years. The same tests are used to both screen for and diagnose diabetes. These tests also detect individuals with prediabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Diabetes and diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: Every patient with diabetes should be established with an ophthalmologist and podiatrist for an initial assessment. In addition, every patient with diabetes should establish care with the nutrition team and diabetes education team. All newly diagnosed diabetes patients should be assessed for albuminuria with spot urinary albumin to creatinine ratio (ACR). If albuminuria is present (urinary ACR > 30mg/g on spot sample), they should be started on angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEI) or Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARB) and titrated up to the maximally tolerated dose (based on blood pressure). An increase in creatinine up to 30% from baseline is expected and shouldn’t lead to discontinuation of ACEI/ARB.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes Mellitus Screening -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for any disease is fruitful when there is benefit from its early diagnosis and intervention. This activity discusses the screening for diabetes mellitus, which is 1 of those diseases that affect millions worldwide. Diabetes Mellitus includes 3 groups of diseases: type 1 diabetes mellitus, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and gestational diabetes

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of hypertension (stage I) requires ambulatory blood pressure reading consistently. Systolic blood pressure (SBP) ≥ 130 mm Hg and/or diastolic blood pressure (DBP) ≥ 80 mm Hg. Patients with SBP 120 to 129 mm Hg and DBP <80 should be categorized as elevated BP and not hypertension. If there is a disparity in categorizing patients due to different values of SBP and DBP, the higher value should be used. For example, someone with BP 117/84 has stage I hypertension. Stage II hypertension is present if SBP ≥ 140 mm Hg and/or DBP ≥ 90 mm Hg. Screening for hypertension should be done at least annually in low-risk populations. For people whose SBP is 120 to 129 mm Hg or have risk factors for hypertension, blood pressure should be measured at least semi-annually.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: There is no clinical recommendation for screening for type 1 diabetes in asymptomatic low-risk individuals. For type 2 diabetes and prediabetes, adult screening should begin at age 45. It should be conducted earlier if BMI ≥25 (BMI ≥ 23 for Asian Americans) and at least one risk factor is present: hypertension, cardiovascular disease, dyslipidemia (low HDL or high triglycerides), polycystic ovary syndrome, physical inactivity, non-White race, severe obesity (BMI ≥40), first degree relative with type 2 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: For diabetes, the most important preventative aspect is identifying the population at increased risk of developing diabetes based on weight, family history, presence of pre-diabetes, typical symptoms, and history of gestational diabetes. Early and frequent screening remains the most important factor for diabetes management. It is important to remember that 90% of adults (18+) with newly diagnosed diabetes are either overweight or obese. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Screening
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes can be diagnosed in either of four ways: Random hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) ≥6.5% Fasting blood sugar ≥126 mg/dl A 2-hr postprandial glucose ≥200 mg/dl after a 75 gm standard oral glucose load Random blood sugar ≥200 mg/dl in a patient with classic symptoms of diabetes (polyuria, polydipsia, and/or polyphagia) or in the patient presenting with a hyperglycemic crisis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2614
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is described as a general clinical state that is interpreted by clinicians (is_interpreted_by) and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications. This indicates that an uncontrolled disease condition determination reflects a concerning overall clinical status and frequently co‑occurs with complicated diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with such uncontrolled conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes). Patients with type 1 diabetes have a heightened risk of severe peripheral vascular disease and foot complications, which can progress to the need for an above‑knee amputation. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians to implement early screening, aggressive glycemic control, and multidisciplinary foot‑care strategies aimed at preventing major limb loss and improving overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1941
retrieved_docs.content: Above reference range is used as an interpretation when evaluating disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that laboratory values exceeding the normal limits may reflect underlying lipid metabolic abnormalities. Within the category of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific subtype, representing conditions characterized by excessive circulating lipoproteins. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset prediabetes) is considered a member of the broader disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical interrelationship between dysglycemia and lipid metabolic disturbances. Understanding these connections assists clinicians in interpreting abnormal lipid panels, identifying hyperlipoproteinemic states, and recognizing the metabolic overlap with early glucose dysregulation for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol levels frequently coexist with this form of diabetes. This relationship underscores the importance of simultaneous screening for lipid abnormalities in patients with type 2 diabetes and integrated management of both cholesterol and glycemic control to improve overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes disorders, pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance, medication contraindications, retinal and peripheral vascular complications—including various levels of lower‑extremity amputation and stump necrosis—and metabolic syndrome, all of which are linked through shared comorbidity classifications. This integrated understanding emphasizes the importance of comprehensive management strategies that address both the primary metabolic disorder and its extensive systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: and therapeutically. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications link specific compounds to their mechanisms, chemical families, and clinical applications, providing a structured framework for endocrine evaluation, diagnostic testing, and comprehensive management of diabetes and related metabolic disorders, with biguanides explicitly identified as a subclass of non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents used to reduce blood glucose in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1794
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) encompasses the specific condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium,” which is a recognized form of diabetes. Diabetes is clinically associated with retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and more specifically with diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ). Both diabetes and diabetic retinopathy are also clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). These connections illustrate how diabetes can lead to multiple organ complications, linking retinal pathology and kidney failure, and emphasize the need for integrated monitoring and management of patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2275
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon, a key metabolic hormone, can be managed with the therapeutic approach identified as “رد فعل تأقي,” while the medication referred to as “ورم جزيري” is contraindicated for use in patients receiving glucagon, indicating that this drug should be avoided to prevent adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a type of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also regarded as a form of hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within the category of disorders of lipid metabolism, conditions such as elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes mellitus (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are included as members. This grouping highlights the close physiological link between lipid and glucose metabolism, indicating that abnormalities in lipid handling often coexist with or contribute to dysglycemia and the development of diabetes. Recognizing that disorders of lipid metabolism belong to broader metabolic disorder categories and encompass hyperlipoproteinemia and early diabetes supports clinicians in performing comprehensive assessments and delivering integrated care for patients with metabolic syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 134
retrieved_docs.content: drugs” both reside under the overarching class of blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins, with metformin as a specific agent within the biguanide subclass. This knowledge base integrates the therapeutic categories of glucose‑lowering and lipid‑lowering agents, highlights fixed‑dose combinations with long‑acting insulins, and underscores important drug‑interaction contraindications relevant to patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2625
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary diabetes mellitus, defined here as secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy and gangrene, is a form of diabetes that arises secondary to another underlying condition and is complicated by severe peripheral vascular disease and tissue necrosis. This underlying secondary diabetes mellitus directly gives rise to several microvascular complications: mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus. The coexistence of diabetic peripheral angiopathy and gangrene further impairs blood flow, heightening the risk and severity of these ocular and renal manifestations. Recognizing that the retinopathies and nephropathy are attributable to the secondary diabetes mellitus, especially when accompanied by peripheral angiopathy and gangrene, guides clinicians to prioritize tight glycemic control, aggressive management of peripheral vascular disease, and early screening for eye and kidney involvement to improve patient outcomes.
